thesis: After this post, a marketer can tag their last 10 videos as get-found or get-chosen and fix the missing half.
firstComment:
Want the audit done for you? Paste this into ChatGPT or Claude (works on the free plan):

You're a B2B YouTube strategist. Here are my last 10 video titles:
[paste titles]
1. Tag each FOUND (answers a search) or CHOSEN (helps a ready buyer pick us: comparison, pricing, objection, proof).
2. Tell me the ratio and which job I'm starving.
3. Suggest 3 CHOSEN video ideas with titles, based on my list.
Return a table (Title | Tag | Why), then the 3 ideas.
postAngle: Every video does one of two jobs: get found or get chosen.
linkedinPost:
Your YouTube channel does 1 of 2 jobs. Most B2B channels only do the first.

I've audited a stack of B2B channels, and the leak is almost always the same.

Every video does one of two jobs:

Get found: it answers a question your buyer is searching. Discovery, SEO, top of funnel. The big view counts live here.

Get chosen: it helps a buyer who already knows the options pick you. Comparisons, pricing, objections, proof. Small numbers, real pipeline.

Most teams pour everything into getting found, because views feel like progress. Then they wonder why a channel with thousands of viewers books 0 calls.

Steal this 30-second audit:
List your last 10 videos. Tag each one.
FOUND = answers a search.
CHOSEN = helps a ready buyer pick you.
8 or more FOUND? That is your leak. Make your next 3 CHOSEN.

Found still matters. It fills the top of the funnel. Just do not let it be the whole funnel.

Think about your best-performing video. Was it built to get found, or to get chosen?
xPost:
Your YouTube channel does 1 of 2 jobs:

get people to find you,
or get ready buyers to choose you.

Most B2B channels obsess over the first.
Then wonder why 10,000 views books 0 calls.

Audit your last 10 videos.
Don't skip the half that closes.

thesis: After this post, a marketer can lead a title with the buyer’s literal search phrase so the right people find it.
firstComment:
Turn one buyer search into 5 titles that actually rank -> https://sellontube.com/tools/youtube-title-generator?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence

Or do it in ChatGPT / Claude (free plan):

You're a B2B YouTube SEO strategist. Topic: [your video]. Buyer: [role].
1. 5 phrases this buyer would likely search (candidates to validate).
2. Turn each into a title: their exact phrase up front + a differentiator tail.
3. Flag buying-intent vs curiosity.
Table: Phrase | Title | Intent. Then the 2 to make first.
Validate the phrases in YouTube autocomplete before filming.
postAngle: Titles written for humans miss the buyer’s search.
linkedinPost:
Your best video is invisible. The title did it.

I've watched genuinely great videos die under clever titles, and it always comes down to one thing.

You wrote the title for a human skimming a feed. But buyers do not skim, they search. They type an exact phrase into YouTube, and a clever title that shares none of those words never shows up.

A great video sits under the title "Churn, Solved." Nobody searches that. Your buyer types "how to reduce SaaS churn." Same video, 2 titles, and only 1 ever gets found.

Steal this title formula:
[the exact phrase your buyer searches] + [your differentiator]

Example:
"how to reduce SaaS churn" + "without dropping your price"
= How to reduce SaaS churn without dropping your price

Boring? A little. But a boring title a buyer searches beats a clever one nobody types, every time.

Look at your best video's title. Would a buyer ever actually type it into search?
xPost:
Your best video is invisible.
The title did it.

You wrote it for someone skimming a feed.
But buyers don't skim. They search.

A clever title nobody types = a video nobody finds.

Lead with the exact phrase they'd search.
Boring beats clever here.

thesis: After this post, a founder can give every video one specific next step so viewers become leads.
firstComment:
Want the CTAs written for you? Paste into ChatGPT or Claude (free):

You're a B2B demand-gen expert. My video teaches: [topic]. I sell: [offer]. Viewer: [role].
Write 3 calls-to-action that fit THIS video (never "visit our site"):
- one comment-bait ("comment X and I'll send the [resource]")
- one low-friction ask (15-min mapping / resource in description)
- one soft next-video nudge
Each 1-2 sentences, said while they're still watching. Rank by likely conversion.
postAngle: Videos with no next step waste the trust they earn.
linkedinPost:
Your best video probably has no idea what to do next. So neither does the viewer.

The best video I ever helped make lost most of its leads in the last 5 seconds. It taught brilliantly, then just ended.

They watch, they nod, they get their answer, and they leave. No next step, no way to raise their hand. You taught them for free and waved goodbye.

Here is why that is expensive. Wyzowl found 89% of people say a video's quality shapes whether they trust a brand. So a good video does the hardest job in sales, earning trust, then strands that trust with nowhere to go.

The fix costs 1 sentence. Give every video a single, specific next step:
Not "check out our website." Too vague.
A resource that matches the video: "I made a 1-page checklist for this. Comment 'checklist' and I'll send it."
Or a low-friction ask: "Want this mapped to your setup? 15 minutes, link in the description."

Worried it feels salesy? It is not a pitch. It is telling someone who just got value where to get more.

Picture your last video's final 10 seconds. What, exactly, do you ask the viewer to do?
xPost:
Your best video has no idea what to do next.
So neither does the viewer.

They watch. They nod. They leave.
You taught them for free and waved goodbye.

Give every video ONE next step.
Specific. Low friction.
Said while they're still watching.

Not "visit our site."

thesis: After this post, a marketer will make an honest comparison video, because those viewers are at the deciding moment.
firstComment: The "vs" videos worth making, and where they fit your funnel -> https://sellontube.com/youtube-vs/?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence
linkedinPost:
One "us vs them" video booked more demos than a year of our thought-leadership. It had the fewest views on the channel.

On a team I ran content for, we had poured months into big-idea posts. Trends, predictions, the odd hot take. Plenty of likes. The sales team never once traced a deal back to any of it.

Then a rep, half joking, asked us to make an honest "us versus the competitor we kept losing to." We hated it. Too salesy, too direct. We made it anyway. It pulled a fraction of the usual views and booked calls every single week.

Here is why. A comparison viewer already has the problem and already knows the options. They are not learning, they are choosing. It was the only video on our channel that showed up while someone was deciding between us and the other guy.

Wyzowl found 83% of video marketers say video has directly increased sales. This is the video that does it, the one nobody wants to make.

Too salesy? The buyer is already comparing you to them. Better they hear it from you, honestly, than only from your competitor.

Think of the competitor your buyers keep naming. Do you have a single honest video about it?
xPost:
One "us vs them" video booked more demos than a year of our thought leadership.

It had the fewest views on the channel.

A comparison viewer isn't learning.
They're choosing.

The video nobody wants to make is the one that closes.

thesis: After this post, a marketer will make videos that answer the questions AI tools cite, to get found inside AI answers.
sourceTitle: Ahrefs — YouTube SEO for AI Search
postAngle: YouTube is the most-cited domain in Google's AI Overviews (Ahrefs).
linkedinPost:
Your buyers have started asking AI instead of searching Google. And the AI keeps quoting one place most B2B teams ignore: YouTube.

Ahrefs dug into this and found something wild: YouTube is the most-cited domain in Google's AI Overviews. So when a buyer asks a question and gets an AI answer, a video is often the source feeding it.

Sit with that. It is no longer just "rank on YouTube so people find you." It is "make videos good enough that the AI answering your buyer's question pulls from yours." A brand-new place to show up, at the exact moment someone is deciding.

Think YouTube is only for reach? It is now a source AI quotes while your buyer is choosing.

Ahrefs lays out a simple 3-step process to get your videos ranking in Google and cited in those AI answers. I won't butcher it secondhand, they explain it properly. Watch it, then go make 1 video that answers a question your buyer would actually ask an AI.

What's the first question your buyer would type into ChatGPT before they'd buy from you?

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_b_cTpLHMTM
xPost:
Your buyers ask AI now, not just Google.

And AI keeps quoting one place B2B teams ignore:

YouTube.

It's the most-cited domain in Google's AI answers.

So the new game isn't just "rank."
It's "be the video the AI quotes" when your buyer is deciding.

thesis: After this post, a marketer can sort videos by the three buyer-search stages and fill the pipeline gap.
firstComment:
Map your funnel in ChatGPT or Claude (free):

You're a B2B YouTube funnel strategist. I sell [product] to [buyer].
Map their search journey: 1. Problem-aware  2. Solution-aware  3. Deciding.
For each stage: 3 real searches they'd type + 1 title that intercepts it.
Table: Stage | Search | Title. Then which stage I under-serve and the first video to fix it.
postAngle: Buyers run problem/solution/vendor searches; channels answer only the first.
linkedinPost:
Your buyer runs 3 searches before they buy. Your channel probably answers 1.

Every "our channel doesn't convert" audit I've done had the same hole: nothing waiting at the deciding stage.

Say you sell project management software. Here is what your buyer types, in order, over weeks:
1. "why do projects keep slipping deadlines." They feel the pain, not the fix yet.
2. "best way to keep remote teams on track." Now they are weighing approaches.
3. "asana vs monday," "monday pricing." They are choosing who to pay.

Almost every channel crams its videos into stage 1, where the big view counts live. But the person typing a stage-3 search has a card out. If none of your videos are waiting there, a competitor takes the sale and you keep the vanity metric.

Wyzowl found 85% of video marketers say video has helped them generate leads. The catch: only if a video exists the moment the lead is ready to raise a hand.

Can't afford 3x the videos? You do not need more videos. You need your next 3 pointed at stages 2 and 3.

Think about your best-performing video. Which of the 3 searches does it actually answer?
xPost:
Your buyer runs 3 searches before they buy.
Your channel probably answers 1.

1. the pain ("why do projects slip?")
2. the approach ("best way to...")
3. the decision ("asana vs monday")

Everyone crams stage 1 for the views.
The sale happens at stage 3.

thesis: After this post, a marketer can pull exact buyer phrases from YouTube autocomplete instead of guessing.
firstComment:
The full autocomplete method, 1 seed phrase into 50 topics -> https://sellontube.com/tools/youtube-autocomplete-keywords?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence

Already have your autocomplete list? Paste it into ChatGPT / Claude (free):

Here are the YouTube autocomplete phrases I pulled for "[seed]":
[paste 15-20]
1. Cluster by intent: problem-aware / solution-aware / deciding.
2. Rank the 8 with the clearest buying intent.
3. Title one video per ranked phrase, leading with the phrase.
Table: Phrase | Stage | Title. Then the 3 to film first.
postAngle: The search bar names the videos your buyers want.
linkedinPost:
Your buyers will tell you exactly what to make. For free. You just have to read YouTube's search bar instead of guessing in a meeting.

I stopped guessing video topics in planning meetings the day I learned this.

Type "cold email" slowly into YouTube. It fills in "cold email templates," "cold email tools," "cold email vs LinkedIn." Those are not guesses. They are real searches, ranked by how many people make them. That is your buyer naming the video to shoot.

The 10-minute version:
1. Type your category plus a buying word: "pricing," "vs," "best... for."
2. Screenshot what autocomplete gives you. Every phrase is a proven, in-demand topic.
3. Keep the ones where the searcher is deciding. Cut the vague ones.
4. Make 1 video per phrase, titled the way it was searched.

Feels too simple? Simple is the point. These are real searches ranked by real demand, not a whiteboard guess.

Do this once and next month's content is planned from real demand. And the people who show up are shopping, not scrolling.

What's the last video you shot on a hunch instead of a search?
xPost:
Your buyers will tell you exactly what to make.
For free.

Type your topic slowly into YouTube search.
Watch it autocomplete:
"...templates"
"...pricing"
"...vs [competitor]"

Those aren't guesses.
They're real searches, ranked by demand.

Stop guessing in meetings.

thesis: After this post, a marketer will make evergreen search videos instead of chasing trends.
linkedinPost:
Chasing trending topics is the fastest way to build a channel nobody searches for next month.

I've watched teams ride a trend to a great week and a dead quarter.

A trend spikes, everyone rushes to post, you catch a week of views. Then the trend dies and takes the video with it. You are left with a library that has an expiry date and a sales team that never felt a thing.

Here is what trends hide: your buyers are not searching what is hot. They are searching what is broken. "How to fix X." "Best tool for Y." "Is Z worth it." Those never trend. They just happen, quietly, every day, forever.

An evergreen video answering one of those keeps getting found for years. A trend video is a fireworks show: bright, loud, gone.

But trends get views. Sure, they do. Rented attention. An evergreen search is an asset you own.

So before you jump on the next hot take, ask a colder question: will anyone search this in 6 months? If not, you are renting attention, not building an asset.

What's one evergreen search your buyers make that you've never made a video for?
xPost:
Chasing trends is the fastest way to build a channel nobody searches for next month.

Your buyers don't search what's hot.
They search what's broken.

"how to fix X"
"best tool for Y"
"is Z worth it"

Those never trend.
They just pay, quietly, for years.

thesis: After this post, a marketer will treat a video as a compounding asset, not a disposable post.
firstComment: The buyer searches worth ranking for -> https://sellontube.com/tools/youtube-seo-tool?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence
postAngle: A ranked video keeps converting for years.
linkedinPost:
A LinkedIn post dies in 48 hours. A YouTube video kept booking us demos 18 months later.

On a team I led, we had videos we had forgotten about, made a year and a half earlier, still pulling demo requests every month. Nobody was promoting them. They ranked for a search buyers make, so they kept getting found and kept converting while we slept.

That is the trade almost nobody weighs. Same afternoon, you make a LinkedIn post or a search-optimized video. The post peaks tonight and is gone by Thursday. The video answering "best tool for remote onboarding" can sit near the top of that search for years.

Wyzowl found 82% of marketers say video gives them a good ROI. This is the mechanism behind that number: pay once, earn for years.

Video feels like more work than a post? It is. Once. Then it earns while you sleep, for years.

So stop grading a video by its first week. Grade it by year 2.

Think of your best video from a year ago. Is it still bringing you anything today?
xPost:
A LinkedIn post dies in 48 hours.

A YouTube video kept booking us demos 18 months later.

Same afternoon to make either one.

One peaks tonight and is gone by Thursday.
The other ranks for a buyer search for years.

Stop grading videos by week 1.
Grade them by year 2.

thesis: After this post, a marketer will optimize for buyer search instead of the watch-time algorithm.
postAngle: The algorithm rewards watch time, not buyer intent.
linkedinPost:
Stop trying to beat the YouTube algorithm. For B2B, you are grinding at a game you do not even want to win.

The algorithm optimizes for 1 thing: watch time. It pushes whatever keeps the most people watching longest. Perfect if you are an entertainer. Poison if you sell software.

Because the video that glues 100,000 casual viewers to their seats is almost never the one that makes a single buyer book a call. Chase watch time and you drift toward broad, snackable content, away from the narrow, unsexy, high-intent videos that actually close deals.

The game worth playing is search. When someone types "best tool for onboarding remote teams," that is a buyer with a hand up. You do not need the algorithm's blessing. You need to be the answer to that exact search.

But the algorithm brings reach. For entertainers, sure. For B2B, reach without intent is just a bigger crowd walking past your booth.

Tiny audience. No shot at viral. Every viewer in-market. For B2B, take that trade every time.

If a video brought you 500 of the right buyers and 0 virality, would your strategy today ever let you make it?
xPost:
Stop trying to beat the YouTube algorithm.

For B2B, you're grinding at a game you don't even want to win.

The algorithm rewards watch time.
Your buyer rewards the exact answer to their search.

Tiny audience. No virality. Every viewer in-market.

Take that trade every time.
